perturbation.original_text: PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree that for the longest period permitted by law each shall hold in strictest confidence and, without the prior written approval of the other party hereto, not to use for their own benefit or the benefit of any party other than the other party hereto, or disclose to any person, firm or corporation other than such party (other than as required by law) any confidential proprietary information concerning the business and affairs of the other party hereto; provided, however, that the foregoing limitations and restrictions shall not apply to information that (i) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by the directors, officers, shareholders, partners, affiliates, employees, agents or advisors of PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, or (ii) is or becomes available to PrimeCall or DeltaThree on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the other party hereto or any of its advisors, agents or affiliates, provided that such source is not known by PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the other party hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: PrimeCall and DeltaThree each agree that they will endeavor to hold confidential proprietary information concerning the business and affairs of the other party hereto; provided, however, that the foregoing limitations and restrictions shall not apply to information that (i) becomes generally available to the public or (ii) becomes available to PrimeCall or DeltaThree on a non-confidential basis from another source.
perturbation.explanation: The modification weakens the obligation to hold information in 'strictest confidence' to simply 'endeavor to hold confidential,' making the obligation less stringent and enforceable. Additionally, removing the phrase 'other than as a result of a disclosure by the directors, officers, shareholders, partners, affiliates, employees, agents or advisors of PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be' from section (i) and removing the phrase 'provided that such source is not known by PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the other party hereto' from section (ii) allows for the potential disclosure of confidential information by company insiders, weakening the overall protection of proprietary information. The clause 'for the longest period permitted by law' is also removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts the New York Trade Secrets Act. The NY law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets and confidential information. Changing 'shall hold in strictest confidence' to 'endeavor to hold confidential' creates an ambiguity as to whether sufficient and reasonable measures will be taken to maintain confidentiality, especially if 'endeavor' is interpreted as a lower standard of care. The removal of the phrase 'other than as a result of a disclosure by the directors, officers, shareholders, partners, affiliates, employees, agents or advisors of PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be' from section (i) and removing the phrase 'provided that such source is not known by PrimeCall or DeltaThree, as the case may be, to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with or other obligation of secrecy to the other party hereto' from section (ii) directly undermines the reasonable efforts required to maintain the secrecy of confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the implied requirements of the New York Trade Secrets Act, which necessitates 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The original contract used the phrase 'shall hold in strictest confidence,' while the modified text uses 'endeavor to hold confidential proprietary information.' This shift weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity as to whether sufficient measures will be taken to maintain confidentiality. The removal of clauses protecting against insider disclosures also weakens protection of proprietary information. Since Snippet 1 returned a 'requested entry could not be found', there is no direct legislative text to quote. However, based on the Law Explanation, the modifications undermine the reasonable efforts required to maintain confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: DeltaThree shall reimburse PrimeCall for all customer service expenses directly related to users of the Calling Card. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the level of customer service shall be subject to the approval of DeltaThree.
perturbation.changed_text: DeltaThree shall reimburse PrimeCall for reasonable customer service expenses vaguely related to users of the Calling Card, based on DeltaThree's general satisfaction.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'all customer service expenses directly related' with 'reasonable customer service expenses vaguely related' introduces ambiguity regarding what expenses are reimbursable. Changing 'subject to the approval of DeltaThree' to 'based on DeltaThree's general satisfaction' makes the standard subjective and unclear, potentially leading to disputes and under-reimbursement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This could potentially contradict New York General Business Law § 349, which prohibits deceptive acts and practices in business. If PrimeCall incurs substantial customer service expenses believing they will be reimbursed, but DeltaThree refuses reimbursement based on vague 'general satisfaction', this could be construed as a deceptive practice if DeltaThree's criteria for 'satisfaction' are not transparent or consistently applied. The ambiguous wording creates an opportunity for unfair practices that could deceive PrimeCall regarding its expected reimbursements, especially if PrimeCall's sales are negatively affected by DeltaThree's underfunding.

(a) Deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state are hereby declared unlawful. (b) Whenever the attorney general shall believe from evidence satisfactory to him that any person, firm, corporation or association or agent or employee thereof has engaged in or is about to engage in any of the acts or practices stated to be unlawful he may bring an action in the name and on behalf of the people of the state of New York to enjoin such unlawful acts or practices and to obtain restitution of any moneys or property obtained directly or indirectly by any such unlawful acts or practices. In such action preliminary relief may be granted under article sixty-three of the civil practice law and rules. (c) Before any violation of this section is sought to be enjoined, the attorney general shall be required to give the person against whom such proceeding is contemplated notice by certified mail and an opportunity to show in writing within five business days after receipt of notice why proceedings should not be instituted against him, unless the attorney general shall find, in any case in which he seeks preliminary relief, that to give such notice and opportunity is not in the public interest. perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts New York General Business Law § 349, which prohibits deceptive acts and practices. By changing 'all customer service expenses directly related' to 'reasonable customer service expenses vaguely related' and 'subject to the approval of DeltaThree' to 'based on DeltaThree's general satisfaction,' the contract introduces ambiguity. This ambiguity creates an opportunity for DeltaThree to unfairly deny reimbursement to PrimeCall based on subjective criteria, which could be construed as a deceptive practice. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2: 'Deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state are hereby declared unlawful.' The modified contract language allows for potentially deceptive reimbursement practices, violating this law.

perturbation.original_text: PrimeCall shall provide DeltaThree with pricing and marketing services in connection with the Calling Card, including without limitation, developing a cost structure for the sale of the Calling Cards, pricing specific routes and determining discount rates. DeltaThree shall be responsible for all costs in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: PrimeCall may offer suggestions regarding pricing and marketing services, subject to DeltaThree's sole discretion. DeltaThree may choose to bear some costs related to these services.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall provide' to 'may offer suggestions' makes PrimeCall's obligation discretionary. Replacing 'DeltaThree shall be responsible for all costs in connection therewith' with 'DeltaThree may choose to bear some costs related to these services' makes the financial obligation vague, creating uncertainty and potentially allowing DeltaThree to avoid covering legitimate expenses. The phrase 'sole discretion' means DeltaThree may choose whatever it sees fit and this can lead to conflicts.
perturbation.law_explanation: This potentially contradicts New York Uniform Commercial Code § 2-305 regarding open price terms. If PrimeCall's marketing and pricing services are considered essential to the Calling Card's sales (a 'sale of goods' scenario under the UCC), then this section of the contract essentially becomes an agreement to agree, with DeltaThree having sole discretion to determine the price and costs, making the agreement unenforceable. The ambiguity around cost allocation would likely render the agreement unenforceable for indefiniteness.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-304 Price Payable in Money, Goods, Realty, or Otherwise up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-306 Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-305 Open Price Term Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account.

Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall provide' to 'may offer suggestions' and 'shall be responsible' to 'may choose to bear' creates ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the contract, making it difficult to determine the price for services. According to UCC § 2-305, the parties can conclude a contract even if the price is not settled, and the price will be a reasonable one if nothing is said about it or if it is left to be agreed upon but the parties fail to agree. However, the changed text introduces uncertainty about whether DeltaThree is actually obligated to pay for PrimeCall's services. This undermines the requirement for good faith in fixing a price, as one party (DeltaThree) has sole discretion, potentially leading to disputes and unenforceability. The original contract clearly defines the responsibilities of both parties, but the altered version leaves the agreement vulnerable to being deemed unenforceable for indefiniteness. The phrase 'sole discretion' creates a loophole that could be challenged in court.
